title: Right Triangles & Trigonometry
blurb: The Pythagorean theorem and its converse; classifying triangles; special right triangles; the SOH-CAH-TOA trig ratios; finding sides; and angles of elevation and depression with inverse trig.
buildsOn: Similarity (Unit 7) — the trig ratios are fixed BECAUSE same-angle right triangles are similar; plus the triangle angle sum (Unit 4) and parallel-line angles (Unit 3).

lessons.title: The Pythagorean Theorem
lessons.concreteComparison: A carpenter checks a corner is truly square by measuring $3$ ft along one edge and $4$ ft along the other: if the diagonal between those marks is exactly $5$ ft, the corner is a perfect right angle. That $3$-$4$-$5$ trick is the Pythagorean theorem doing real work.
lessons.objective: Use a² + b² = c² to find a missing side of a right triangle, and recognize Pythagorean triples.
concept: In a **right triangle**, the two shorter sides are the **legs** ($a$ and $b$) and the longest side — opposite the right angle — is the **hypotenuse** ($c$). They satisfy the **Pythagorean theorem**: $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
To find the **hypotenuse**: $c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$. To find a missing **leg**: $a = \sqrt{c^2 - b^2}$.
A **Pythagorean triple** is three whole numbers that fit exactly: $3, 4, 5$; $5, 12, 13$; $8, 15, 17$ — and any multiple, like $6, 8, 10$.
The hypotenuse is always the **longest** side and sits **opposite** the right angle — never a leg.
lessons.keyIdea: In a right triangle, legs a, b and hypotenuse c satisfy a² + b² = c². Find the hypotenuse with √(a²+b²) and a leg with √(c²−b²). Whole-number solutions are Pythagorean triples.

facts.title: Pythagorean theorem
facts.explanation: Legs squared add to the hypotenuse squared.

workedExamples.statement: A right triangle has legs $6$ and $8$. Find the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has hypotenuse $13$ and one leg $5$. Find the other leg.

commonMistakes.text: Using the hypotenuse formula to find a leg.
commonMistakes.correction: For a **leg**, SUBTRACT: $a = \sqrt{c^2 - b^2}$. Only the hypotenuse uses a sum.

commonMistakes.text: Treating a leg as the hypotenuse.
commonMistakes.correction: The hypotenuse is the **longest** side, opposite the right angle. Put the largest value as $c$.

tryIt.statement: Hypotenuse $17$, one leg $8$. Find the other leg.

steps.title: The sides of a right triangle
beats: The two sides forming it are the **legs** ($a$, $b$).
The side across from the right angle — the longest — is the **hypotenuse** ($c$).
A fixed relationship ties them together.

steps.title: a² + b² = c², as areas
steps.teach: A square is built on each side. The area on leg $a$ plus the area on leg $b$ always exactly fills the area on the hypotenuse $c$. Change the legs — the two smaller squares always add to the biggest. That's $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.

steps.title: Squares add up
beats: **$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$** for every right triangle.
The hypotenuse is always the longest side.

steps.title: Find the hypotenuse
steps.intro: Add the squared legs, then square-root.

steps.title: Subtract instead of add
beats: If you know the hypotenuse and one leg, rearrange.
Subtract the known leg's square from the hypotenuse's square.

steps.title: The rope with 12 knots
steps.body: Ancient Egyptian surveyors carried a loop of rope with $12$ evenly spaced knots. Pulled taut into a $3$-$4$-$5$ triangle, it forced a perfect right angle — no protractor needed — letting them reset field boundaries after the Nile's floods. The Pythagorean triple was a tool long before it was a theorem.

steps.title: Find c
steps.prompt: A right triangle has legs $20$ and $21$. What is the hypotenuse?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$** in every right triangle; $c$ is the hypotenuse (longest, opposite the right angle).
**Pythagorean triples** like $3$-$4$-$5$ and $5$-$12$-$13$ are whole-number right triangles.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find hypotenuses and legs with the Pythagorean theorem.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The hypotenuse of a right triangle is always —

steps.title: You've finished "The Pythagorean Theorem"
steps.body: You can find any side of a right triangle. Next: running the theorem backwards to TEST for a right angle — and to tell acute from obtuse.

lessons.title: The Converse & Classifying Triangles
lessons.concreteComparison: The $3$-$4$-$5$ rope works because the theorem runs both ways: if three sides HAPPEN to satisfy $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, the triangle is forced to be right. And when they don't quite fit, the size of the gap tells you whether the triangle is acute or obtuse.
lessons.objective: Use the converse of the Pythagorean theorem to test whether three sides form a right triangle, and classify a triangle by comparing a² + b² to c².
concept: **Converse of the Pythagorean Theorem**: if the three sides satisfy $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ (with $c$ the **longest**), then the triangle is a **right** triangle.
If $a^2 + b^2 > c^2$: the longest side is 'too short' to open a right angle, so the triangle is **acute**.
If $a^2 + b^2 < c^2$: the longest side is 'too long', so the triangle is **obtuse**.
Always compare using the **longest** side as $c$ — that's the side opposite the largest angle.
lessons.keyIdea: Test three sides with the longest as c: a²+b² = c² → right; a²+b² > c² → acute; a²+b² < c² → obtuse.

facts.title: Converse
facts.explanation: If the sides fit, the triangle is right-angled.

facts.title: Classify
facts.explanation: Compare a²+b² to c² (c the longest side).

workedExamples.statement: Do sides $6, 8, 10$ form a right triangle?

commonMistakes.text: Comparing with the wrong side as $c$.
commonMistakes.correction: Always square the **longest** side for $c$. Using a shorter side scrambles the test.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up which way acute vs obtuse goes.
commonMistakes.correction: $a^2 + b^2$ **bigger** than $c^2$ → **acute**; **smaller** → **obtuse**. (Equal is the right-angle boundary.)

steps.title: The theorem in reverse
beats: Forward: a right triangle **gives** $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
Backward (**converse**): if $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$, the triangle **must be** right.
So three side lengths alone can prove a right angle.
And near-misses tell you acute or obtuse.

steps.title: When the squares match exactly
steps.teach: When $a^2 + b^2$ exactly equals $c^2$, the two leg-squares fill the hypotenuse-square with nothing left over — a right angle. Try $3, 4$ (fills to $25 = 5^2$) versus other legs.

steps.title: Compare a² + b² to c²
beats: Equal is the exact boundary between acute and obtuse.

steps.title: Right triangle or not?
steps.intro: Check whether a² + b² = c² (c longest).

steps.title: Is it right-angled?
steps.intro: a² + b² vs c², longest as c.

steps.title: Read the gap
beats: If it's not exactly right, the comparison classifies it.
$a^2 + b^2 < c^2$: the longest side wins → **obtuse**.
Always with $c$ as the longest side.

steps.title: How a builder finds a wall out of square
steps.body: Measure a wall's diagonal and its two sides, then check $a^2 + b^2$ against the diagonal squared. If the diagonal is too long, the corner is obtuse (splayed open); too short, it's acute (pinched). The converse turns a tape measure into a right-angle detector.

steps.title: Classify it
steps.prompt: A triangle has sides $8, 9, 10$. It is —

steps.title: What you learned
points: Always test with the **longest** side as $c$.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's test for right angles and classify by comparison.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Which side do you square as $c$ in the test?
problems.explanation: The longest — opposite the biggest angle.

problems.prompt: If $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ exactly, the triangle is —
problems.explanation: The converse gives a right triangle.

steps.title: You've finished "The Converse & Classifying Triangles"
steps.body: You can test any three sides and classify the triangle. Next: two right triangles so common their side ratios are worth memorizing — the 45-45-90 and 30-60-90.

lessons.title: Special Right Triangles
lessons.concreteComparison: Cut a square in half along its diagonal and you get a 45-45-90 triangle; cut an equilateral triangle down the middle and you get a 30-60-90. These two shapes appear so often — in drafting, tiling, and roof framing — that their side ratios are worth memorizing so you never re-derive them.
lessons.objective: Use the 45-45-90 ratio (leg : leg : leg√2) and the 30-60-90 ratio (x : x√3 : 2x) to find sides without recomputing.
concept: A **45-45-90** triangle (half a square) is isosceles: the two legs are equal, and the **hypotenuse is a leg $\times \sqrt2$**. Leg $5 \Rightarrow$ hypotenuse $5\sqrt2$.
A **30-60-90** triangle (half an equilateral triangle): with the **short leg** (opposite $30^\circ$) $= x$, the **long leg** (opposite $60^\circ$) $= x\sqrt3$, and the **hypotenuse** $= 2x$.
Both ratios come straight from the Pythagorean theorem — they're just worth memorizing so a calculation becomes a lookup.
Match each side to its position: in 30-60-90 the hypotenuse is **twice the short leg**, and the long leg is the short leg times $\sqrt3$.
lessons.keyIdea: 45-45-90: legs equal, hypotenuse = leg·√2. 30-60-90: short leg x, long leg x√3, hypotenuse 2x. Memorize the ratios instead of re-deriving.

facts.explanation: Equal legs; hypotenuse is a leg times √2.
Short leg x, long leg x√3, hypotenuse 2x.

workedExamples.statement: A 45-45-90 triangle has legs of $7$. Find the hypotenuse.
A 30-60-90 triangle has short leg $6$. Find the hypotenuse and long leg.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying the wrong leg by √3 in a 30-60-90.
commonMistakes.correction: The **long** leg is the **short** leg times $\sqrt3$. Start from the short leg (opposite $30^\circ$).

commonMistakes.text: Using √2 for a 30-60-90 or √3 for a 45-45-90.
commonMistakes.correction: $\sqrt2$ belongs to **45-45-90**; $\sqrt3$ (and the doubling) belong to **30-60-90**. Don't swap them.

tryIt.statement: A 30-60-90 triangle has short leg $5$. Hypotenuse?

steps.title: Two shapes worth memorizing
beats: Some right triangles show up again and again.
**45-45-90**: half a square — two equal legs.
Their side ratios never change, only the size.

steps.title: 45-45-90: leg, leg, leg√2
steps.teach: Both legs are equal; the hypotenuse is always a leg times $\sqrt2 \approx 1.41$. Change the size $x$ — the shape and ratio stay the same, which is why you can just memorize it.

steps.title: Multiply a leg by √2
beats: The two legs are equal (it's isosceles).

problems.prompt: In a 45-45-90, the two legs are —

steps.title: 30-60-90: x, x√3, 2x
steps.teach: The short leg (opposite $30^\circ$) is $x$, the hypotenuse is $2x$, and the long leg (opposite $60^\circ$) is $x\sqrt3 \approx 1.73x$. Resize it — the $1 : \sqrt3 : 2$ ratio holds.

examples.prompt: Short leg $6$. Hypotenuse and long leg?

problems.prompt: In a 30-60-90, the hypotenuse is — the short leg.

steps.title: Why drafting sets have two triangles
steps.body: A classic drafting kit holds exactly two set squares: a 45-45-90 and a 30-60-90. Between them they draw angles of $30, 45, 60, 90$ — and, by stacking, $15, 75, 105$ and more — which is why these two special triangles are the workhorses of technical drawing.

steps.title: Special ratio
steps.prompt: A 30-60-90 triangle has a short leg of $9$. What is its hypotenuse?
steps.explanation: The hypotenuse is twice the short leg: $2 \cdot 9 = 18$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Memorize the ratios — $\sqrt2$ for the isosceles one, doubling & $\sqrt3$ for the other.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find sides in 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangles from the ratios.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The $\sqrt2$ ratio belongs to a — triangle.
In a 30-60-90, the LONG leg is opposite the — angle.

steps.title: You've finished "Special Right Triangles"
steps.body: You can read off the sides of the two special triangles instantly. Next: trigonometry — sine, cosine, and tangent — which handles EVERY right triangle, not just the special two.

lessons.title: Trigonometric Ratios (SOH-CAH-TOA)
lessons.concreteComparison: Every right triangle with the same acute angle is similar (Unit 7), so its side RATIOS are locked in by that angle alone. Trigonometry just gives those three ratios names — sine, cosine, tangent — turning an angle into a number you can compute with.
lessons.objective: Name the sides of a right triangle relative to an angle (opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse) and compute sine, cosine, and tangent from the side lengths (SOH-CAH-TOA).
concept: For an acute angle $\theta$ in a right triangle, label the three sides **relative to $\theta$**: the **opposite** side (across from $\theta$), the **adjacent** side (next to $\theta$, not the hypotenuse), and the **hypotenuse** (opposite the right angle).
**SOH-CAH-TOA**: $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$.
Because all right triangles with the same acute angle are **similar**, these ratios depend only on the angle — not the triangle's size. That is why $\sin30^\circ$ is one fixed number.
To compute a ratio: identify opp / adj / hyp for the angle, then divide.
lessons.keyIdea: Relative to an acute angle θ: sin θ = opp/hyp, cos θ = adj/hyp, tan θ = opp/adj. The ratios depend only on the angle because same-angle right triangles are similar.

facts.title: Why it's fixed
facts.explanation: The ratio depends only on the angle, not the size.

workedExamples.statement: In a right triangle, the side opposite $\theta$ is $6$ and the hypotenuse is $10$. Find $\sin\theta$.
The side adjacent to $\theta$ is $4$, the opposite is $3$. Find $\tan\theta$.

commonMistakes.correction: The **adjacent** is the leg next to $\theta$. The **hypotenuse** (opposite the right angle) is never the adjacent side.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up which ratio is which.
commonMistakes.correction: Chant **SOH-CAH-TOA**: Sine = Opp/Hyp, Cosine = Adj/Hyp, Tangent = Opp/Adj.

steps.title: Name the sides by the angle
beats: Pick an acute angle $\theta$ in a right triangle.
**Adjacent**: the leg next to $\theta$ (not the hypotenuse).
**Hypotenuse**: the longest side, across from the right angle.

steps.title: The three ratios, live
steps.teach: For the angle $\theta$, watch $\sin$, $\cos$, and $\tan$ — each is a ratio of two sides (opp/hyp, adj/hyp, opp/adj). Change $\theta$ and the ratios change; keep $\theta$ fixed and they stay put no matter the size.

steps.title: Opp and adj swap
beats: A right triangle has two acute angles.
A side that is **opposite** one is **adjacent** to the other.
So $\sin$ of one angle equals $\cos$ of the other.
Always name the sides relative to the angle you're using.

steps.title: From a 3-4-5 triangle
steps.intro: opp, adj, hyp = 3, 4, 5 for the angle at the ‘4’ vertex.

examples.prompt: In a 3-4-5 right triangle, for the angle with opp $3$, adj $4$: find $\cos$.

steps.title: Trig ratios are older than algebra
steps.body: Astronomers were tabulating chord and sine values over $1{,}500$ years ago — long before modern algebra existed — to predict the positions of the sun and planets. Every value in those tables is just opp/hyp for some angle, the same SOH you're learning now.

steps.title: Pick the ratio
steps.prompt: The side opposite $\theta$ is $7$ and the adjacent side is $24$. What is $\tan\theta$?

steps.title: What you learned
points: The ratios depend only on the **angle** — because same-angle right triangles are **similar**.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's identify sides and compute sine, cosine, and tangent.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The ratios of a right triangle depend on —

steps.title: You've finished "Trigonometric Ratios"
steps.body: You can compute sine, cosine, and tangent from the sides. Next: use them the other way — knowing an angle and a side to find a missing side.

lessons.title: Finding Missing Sides with Trig
lessons.concreteComparison: Know one side and one angle of a right triangle and trig hands you the rest — how high a ladder reaches, how far a ramp rises, how wide a river is. Pick the ratio that links what you know to what you want, and solve.
lessons.objective: Use sine, cosine, or tangent to find a missing side of a right triangle from a known side and angle, including the exact values at 30°, 45°, and 60°.
concept: Choose the ratio that connects the **known side**, the **unknown side**, and the angle: **opp & hyp** → sine, **adj & hyp** → cosine, **opp & adj** → tangent.
Set up the equation, e.g. $\sin\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, substitute the known values, and solve for the unknown.

At the special angles the values are exact: $\sin30^\circ = \tfrac12$, $\cos60^\circ = \tfrac12$, $\tan45^\circ = 1$, $\sin45^\circ = \cos45^\circ = \tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$, and $\sin60^\circ = \cos30^\circ = \tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$.
For any other angle you read the ratio from a calculator, then solve the same way.
lessons.keyIdea: To find a missing side, pick the ratio (sin/cos/tan) linking the known side, unknown side, and angle; substitute and solve. The 30°/45°/60° values are exact.

facts.title: Pick the ratio
facts.explanation: Match the two sides involved to the right ratio.

workedExamples.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $10$ and an angle of $30^\circ$. Find the side opposite the $30^\circ$ angle.
A ramp rises from the ground at $45^\circ$; its horizontal run (adjacent) is $8$. How high does it rise (opposite)?

commonMistakes.text: Choosing a ratio that uses a side you don't have.
commonMistakes.correction: Pick the ratio built from the **two sides in play** (the known and the unknown). If you have opp and want hyp, use **sine**.
$\sin30 = \cos60 = \tfrac12$, $\tan45 = 1$. Knowing these turns a special-angle problem into arithmetic.

steps.title: One side, one angle, find the rest
beats: Pick the ratio that links those two sides to the angle.

steps.title: The ratio links two sides
steps.teach: For angle $\theta$: $\sin$ links opp & hyp, $\cos$ links adj & hyp, $\tan$ links opp & adj. Read off which two sides your problem involves, then use that ratio.

steps.title: Match before you compute
beats: First identify the sides **relative to the given angle**.
Decide which is known and which you want.
Then substitute the exact value (or calculator value) and solve.

steps.title: How tall is that mountain?
steps.body: Surveyors find the height of a peak they can't climb by measuring a base distance and the angle up to the summit, then computing height $= $ distance $\times \tan(\text{angle})$. The same one-line trig fixes the height of trees, towers, and clouds — anything too tall to reach with a tape.

steps.title: Find the side
steps.prompt: A right triangle has hypotenuse $14$ and a $30^\circ$ angle. The side opposite the $30^\circ$ angle is —

steps.title: What you learned
points: Pick the ratio linking the **known** and **unknown** sides: sin (opp·hyp), cos (adj·hyp), tan (opp·adj).
Substitute and solve; the **special angles** $30, 45, 60$ have exact values.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find missing sides using sine, cosine, and tangent.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: To find the hypotenuse from the opposite side and the angle, use —

steps.title: You've finished "Finding Missing Sides with Trig"
steps.body: You can find any side from an angle and a side. Last stop: finding the ANGLE itself with inverse trig, and the elevation/depression problems that use it.

lessons.title: Angles of Elevation & Depression
lessons.concreteComparison: Look up at the top of a building and your line of sight makes an angle of ELEVATION with the horizontal; look down from a cliff to a boat and it's an angle of DEPRESSION. With one distance and one height, trig runs backward to find that angle — using inverse sine, cosine, and tangent.
lessons.objective: Find an unknown angle with inverse trig (arcsin, arccos, arctan), and solve angle-of-elevation and angle-of-depression problems.
concept: To find an **angle** from a known ratio, use the **inverse** trig functions: if $\tan\theta = \tfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$, then $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\big(\tfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}\big)$ (and likewise $\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$).
An **angle of elevation** is measured **upward** from the horizontal to a line of sight; an **angle of depression** is measured **downward** from the horizontal.
The angle of depression from a high point equals the angle of elevation from the low point — they are **alternate interior angles** between two horizontal (parallel) lines (Unit 3).
Set up the right triangle (height = opposite, ground distance = adjacent), then use $\tan$ or its inverse to find the side or the angle.
lessons.keyIdea: Inverse trig (arcsin/arccos/arctan) turns a side ratio into an angle. Angles of elevation (up) and depression (down) are equal alternate interior angles; model the situation as a right triangle and apply tan or its inverse.

facts.title: Inverse trig
facts.explanation: Undo the ratio to recover the angle.

facts.title: Elevation = depression
facts.explanation: Between parallel horizontals, the two angles match.

workedExamples.statement: From $10$ m away, a flagpole's top is level with a $45^\circ$ line of sight ($\text{opp} = 10$, $\text{adj} = 10$). Find the angle of elevation.
A ramp rises $1$ m over a horizontal run of $\sqrt3$ m. Find its angle of elevation.

commonMistakes.text: Using $\sin$ instead of $\sin^{-1}$ to find an angle.
commonMistakes.correction: To go from a **ratio to an angle**, use the **inverse** ($\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$). Plain $\sin$ goes the other way.
Both elevation and depression are measured from the **horizontal**, not the vertical.

steps.title: From a ratio back to an angle
beats: **Inverse trig** turns a **ratio** back into an **angle**.

steps.title: Read the angle from the ratio
steps.teach: For each angle $\theta$, the readout shows its ratios. Inverse trig runs this backward: given a ratio like $\tan\theta = 1$, it returns the angle ($45^\circ$). Match the ratio to find the angle.

steps.title: Use the inverse
beats: The special ratios give exact angles: $\tan^{-1}(1) = 45^\circ$.

problems.prompt: To find an angle from a ratio you use —

steps.title: Up and down from the horizontal
beats: **Elevation**: look **up** — angle from the horizontal to the line of sight.
From a high point down to a low point and back up: the two angles are **equal** (alternate interior angles between parallel horizontals, Unit 3).
Model it as a right triangle: height = opposite, ground = adjacent.

steps.title: Elevation problems
steps.intro: Model a right triangle, use tan or its inverse.

examples.prompt: From $10$ m away, the top of a pole needs a $45^\circ$ look-up; the pole is $10$ m tall. Confirm the elevation angle.
A kite string $20$ m long makes a $30^\circ$ elevation. How high is the kite?

steps.title: Elevation & depression
steps.intro: Set up the triangle, then solve.

problems.prompt: Height $6$, ground distance $6$. Angle of elevation?
The angle of depression from a cliff to a boat equals the boat's angle of — to the cliff top.
String $12$ m at $30^\circ$ elevation. Kite height?

steps.title: Pilots and the glide slope
steps.body: An aircraft on a standard approach follows a $3^\circ$ angle of depression to the runway — gentle enough for a smooth landing. Air-traffic and instrument-landing systems are built around this trig: distance out times $\tan(3^\circ)$ gives exactly how high the plane should be at each point.

steps.title: Find the angle
steps.prompt: A ramp rises $5$ m over a horizontal run of $5$ m. What is its angle of elevation?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Inverse trig** ($\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$) turns a ratio back into an **angle**.
**Elevation** is up from the horizontal, **depression** is down; the two are **equal** (alternate interior angles).
Model the situation as a right triangle, then use $\tan$ (or its inverse) to find a side or the angle.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find angles with inverse trig and solve elevation/depression problems.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Elevation and depression angles between two parallel horizontals are —

steps.title: You've finished "Angles of Elevation & Depression"
steps.body: You can find angles with inverse trig and solve real elevation and depression problems — the finale of Right Triangles & Trigonometry. Next unit: circles, where angles and arcs meet.
